english: 1. Glr., Pth., byi byéd-pa (byi byed pa) to commit adultery or rape of females, byi-čád (byi chad) punishment for it. — 2. v. byi-ba (byi ba). — 3. Pur. *bi* bird, cf. byiu (byi'u).
a medicine Med.
or dur (dur), 1. n. of an animal, inhabiting caves S.g.; byi-dur-ma (byi dur ma) Sik. porcupine. — 2. spine of a porcupine or a hedgehog Sch.
a kind of silk stuff? Wdk.
v. byi-túr (byi tur).

the wiping, cleaning; pýag-bdár żés-pa byi-dór-gyi las dei min (phyag bdar zhes pa byi dor gyi las de'i min) the word pýag-bdár (phyag bdar) denotes the act of cleaning Lex.; commonly byi-dór byéd-pa (byi dor byed pa) e.g. gnás-su (gnas su) to clean, to sweep a place Dzl.; spiritually; to cleanse one's thoughts Mil.; byi-bdár byéd-pa (byi bdar byed pa) Dzl. to dress, trim, decorate one's self, to make one's self smart.
Sch. bosom. — 2. W. male-cat, tom-cat.

I. sbst. B. and C.; col. C. *jhi-tsi* Ld., Pur. *bi-tse*, Ld., Lh. *sa-bi-li(g)*, rat, mouse, and various other animals: byi-ba-rkaṅ-riṅ (byi ba rkang ring) Sch. rabbit (?); dṅúl-byi (dngul byi) Sch. white rabbit. — byi-dkár (byi dkar) Sch. white hare. — byi-kúṅ (byi khung) mouse-hole. — byi-rdo (byi rdo) Sch. rat's-bane, arsenic. — byi-ldém (byi ldem) mouse-trap. — byi-nág (byi nag) Sch. fitchet, polecat. — byi-prúg (byi phrug) young mouse. — byi-brún (byi brun) Dzl. mouse-dung. — byi-blá (byi bla) v. sub byi-la (byi la). — byi-tsáṅ (byi tshang) mouse-nest, mouse-hole. — byi-tsér (byi tsher) medicinal herb Med. — byi-₀dzin (byi 'dzin) Cs. mouse-trap; byi-bzúṅ (byi bzung) Lt., *bi-zúm* W., etymol. id.; but applied to that troublesome plant, the bur (burdock), which is stuck into mouse-holes, to fasten in the skin of the mice. — byi-loṅ (byi long) etym. blind-mouse Sch. mole. — II. vb.: byi-ba byéd-pa (byi ba byed pa) Cs., = byi byéd-pa (byi byed pa) 1. to mouse; to steal, to pilfer. 2. to commit adultery. — III. pf. of ₀byi-ba ('byi ba) q. v.; byi-ba spu (byi ba spu), Sch.. hair that has fallen off.
Lex.; Sch. little child, infant, = byis-pa (byis pa).
n. of one of the lunar mansions, v. rgyu-skár (rgyu skar).
Cs. = tabs (thabs), manner, way, method.
coral, frq., also byú-ru (byu ru); byi-ru mdog (byi ru mdog) light red Glr.
medicinal plant Med.

B., W. *bi-la, bi-li* (Hind. *billa*), cat; byi-lai brun (byi la'i brun), cat's dung Lt.; byi-bla (byi bla) Wdk. id.? In the latter work it is mentioned as the name of a certain monster, whilst byi-blai rgyal-mtsán (byi bla'i rgyal mtshan) is an attribute of the gods, resembling a flag with a cat's head at the top.
Wdk.?
Wdn.?
v. byíṅ-ba (bying ba).
S.g. n. of an animal (?).

1. Cs. general, common. — 2. Sch. hidden, concealed. — 3. Cs. root. The word seems to be a secondary form of spyi (spyi) and dbyiṅs (dbyings), yet in various passages of medical works none of the above meanings is applicable.

1. pomp, splendour, magnificence, e.g. of kings; byin-čé-bar bżúgs-pa (byin che bar bzhugs pa) to be enthroned in great splendour Dzl.; yzi-brjid daṅ byin čé (gzi brjid dang byin che) Dzl., mtu daṅ byin (mthu dang byin) Dzl.; byin-čán (byin can) magnificent, splendid, brilliant, byin-méd (byin med) the contrary. — 2. blessing, a bestowing of blessings, a power working for good, byin-báb (byin bab) Lex., -páb (-phab) Sch.: conferring blessings (?), bcòm-ldan-₀dás-kyi byin-gyis (bcom ldan 'das kyi byin gyis) by the blessing, the miraculous power of Buddha; yet also applied to devils, v. below; most frq. byin-gyis rlób-pa (byin gyis rlob pa), pf. brlabs (brlabs), ft. brlab (brlab), imp. rlobs (rlobs), to bless, mi (mi) a person, sa-yzi (sa gzhi) a place Mil., also followed by the termin.: séms-čan-gyi sdug-bsṅál żi-bar byin-gyis rlobs (sems can gyi sdug bsngal zhi bar byin gyis rlobs) grant thy blessing, that the misery of beings may be assuaged Mil.; bu mťun-rkyén ₀dzóm-bar byin-gyis rlobs (bu mthun rkyen 'dzom bar byin gyis rlobs) bless the son, that all happiness may be accumulated on him Mil.; rgyud gnyén-po bzáṅ-bar (rgyud gnyen po bzang bar) bless my soul, that it may be an efficient help (to these people) Mil.; relative to devils: log- (log -)
